1: Le-khol ze-man ve-et le-khol-chefetz tahat hashamayim — 'For everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven.' The poem of ch. 3 opens with two near-synonyms: ze-man (H2165, an appointed time/fixed time) and et (H6256, a time/occasion). Ze-man is the more precise, carrying the sense of a set appointment (related to zamam, to plan/purpose). Chefetz (H2656) means 'purpose/delight/desire' — what one delights in or intends. The verse frames the poem: it is not randomness but a structured schedule of appointments that governs all activity. The question the poem poses is who sets that schedule.
2: Et laledet ve-et lamut — 'A time to be born, and a time to die.' The 14-pair poem (vv.2-8) uses a merism structure: naming the two extremes implies all that lies between. Birth and death are the ultimate brackets of human life. Et lata'at ve-et la'akor natu'a — 'a time to plant, and a time to uproot.' The agricultural verbs shift the focus: if birth/death are biological facts, planting/uprooting are volitional — human decisions about when to invest and when to abandon. Both pairs fall within the ze-man/et structure: all human decisions, not just biological events, have their appointed time.
3: Et laharog ve-et lirpo — 'A time to kill, and a time to heal.' The verb harag (to kill/slay) is used for both murder and legitimate killing; context determines the moral valence. The Teacher does not categorize: the poem is descriptive of the range of human activities, not prescriptive of which are justified. Et lifrotz ve-et livnot — 'a time to break down, and a time to build up.' The pair describes demolition and construction — both are valid activities within the appointed seasons. The poem does not say which is preferable in the abstract; each has its time.
4: Et livkot ve-et liskhok — 'A time to weep, and a time to laugh.' The emotional pairs: grief and joy, mourning and dancing. Jesus cites this kind of wisdom (implicitly) when he says 'Blessed are those who mourn' (Matt 5:4) and 'Blessed are you who weep now, for you shall laugh' (Luke 6:21). The Teacher observes the structure of appointed emotional seasons; Jesus announces the eschatological reversal that the Teacher, 'under the sun,' cannot see. Et safod ve-et rekhod — 'a time to mourn, and a time to dance.' Safad (to mourn/beat the breast) and raqad (to dance/leap) are the emotional extremes of communal response — mourning at funerals, dancing at festivals.
5: Et le-hashlikh avanim ve-et knos avanim — 'A time to throw away stones, and a time to gather stones.' This pair has generated the most interpretive debate in the poem — the practice of throwing and gathering stones is not self-evidently meaningful. Ancient interpretations include: (1) agricultural land clearing (throwing stones from a field) and gathering them for building; (2) a euphemism for sexual activity (rabbinic); (3) military sieges (throwing stones at fortified cities) and reconstruction after them. The ambiguity is likely intentional — it resists resolution, illustrating the poem's point about inscrutable timing. Et lavkot ve-et lirhod — 'a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing' — physical intimacy has its appointed time.
6: Et levaqesh ve-et le'abed — 'A time to seek, and a time to lose.' The pair contrasts active seeking (baqash, to seek/search) with passive loss (abad, to perish/be lost). The choice to seek and the fact of loss both fall within appointed seasons. Et lishmor ve-et le-hashlikh — 'a time to keep, and a time to throw away.' The pair of shamar (to keep/guard — the same verb as keeping covenant, Exod 20:6) and hashlikh (to throw/cast away) extends the stone-gathering/throwing pair: sometimes what one has faithfully kept must be surrendered.
7: Et li-qro'a ve-et litfor — 'A time to tear, and a time to sew.' Tearing garments was the ancient gesture of grief (Gen 37:29; Job 1:20); sewing them represented the end of mourning. The pair encapsulates the rhythm of grief and recovery. Et la-hachshot ve-et le-dabber — 'a time to keep silence, and a time to speak.' Wisdom literature consistently values the discipline of silence: 'He who restrains his words has knowledge' (Prov 17:27). But silence and speech each have their appointed time — the wisdom is in discerning which the moment calls for.
8: Et le'ehov ve-et lisno — 'A time to love, and a time to hate.' The climactic final pair of human relationships. Love and hate are presented without moral commentary: the poem describes the full range of human relational experience as having an appointed time structure. Et milchamah ve-et shalom — 'a time for war, and a time for peace.' The social/political level: war and peace are not merely human choices but fall within the structured seasons of the cosmos. The poem closes at the political macro-level after opening with the biological micro-level (birth and death), encompassing the full range of human experience.
9: Mah-yitron ha-oseh ba'asher hu amel — 'What gain does the worker have from his toil?' The question of 1:3 (mah yitron) recurs after the poem — but now the context is different. The poem has shown that all activities have their appointed time; the question returns: if all things have their time and everything cycles, what lasting advantage accumulates? The question is answered in vv.10-15: there is no yitron from the worker's perspective, but God's perspective gives meaning to what appears cyclical and futile.
10: Ra'iti et-ha-inyan asher natan Elohim livnei ha-adam la'anot bo — 'I have seen the business that God has given to the children of man to be occupied with.' The word inyan recurs (cf. 1:13): 'unhappy business.' The repetition links this reflection to the opening investigation: the same inyan that God assigned (1:13) is the subject of continued observation. The Teacher's empirical method — seeing, observing, examining — governs his procedure throughout.
11: Et-hakol asah yafeh be-ito — 'He has made everything beautiful in its time.' The word yafeh (beautiful/appropriate) is the same root as the superlative of the Song of Songs (1:15-16: 'you are beautiful'). God's making of 'everything beautiful in its time' affirms the goodness of the appointed schedule — each thing is beautiful precisely when it occurs in its proper time. Gam et-ha-olam natan be-libbam — 'Also, he has put eternity in the human heart.' Olam (H5769) — normally 'forever/eternity/age' — is placed 'in the human heart' as a divine implantation. This is the theological key to why the Teacher's investigation is frustrated: humans have an awareness of eternity (olam) but cannot 'find out what God has done from beginning to end.' The olam-capacity creates the longing; the mortality creates the frustration. The NT's resolution is resurrection: in Christ, the olam-longing is met eschatologically.
12: Yada'ti ki ein tov bam ki im-lismoch ve-la'asot tov be-chayyav — 'I know that there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and to do good throughout their life.' The carpe diem counsel now includes an ethical dimension: ve-la'asot tov ('and to do good'). Enjoyment and goodness are paired — the positive counsel is not hedonism but joyful moral living. The addition of 'do good' distinguishes Ecclesiastes' counsel from mere pleasure-seeking: the appointed seasons include seasons for ethical action, not just personal enjoyment. Be-chayyav — 'throughout his life' — the temporal scope: not a single moment of pleasure but a stance toward the whole of life.
13: Ve-gam kol-ha-adam sheyokhal ve-shata ve-ra'ah tov be-khol amalo — 'And that everyone should eat and drink and take pleasure in his toil.' The formula from 2:24 recurs with slight variation: 'every person' (kol-ha-adam) broadens the counsel from the singular experiment to a universal principle. Matat Elohim hi — 'this is God's gift.' Matat (H4991, gift) from natan (to give) — the capacity for enjoyment is a divine donation, not a human achievement. The passive construction 'is God's gift' is stronger than 2:24's 'from the hand of God': the gift character of enjoyment is now stated as a theological proposition, not just an observation.
14: Yada'ti ki kol-asher ya'aseh ha'Elohim hu yih-yeh le'olam — 'I know that whatever God does endures forever.' Le'olam (forever) — God's acts are characterized by the olam permanence that human acts lack. Ein le-hosif alav ve-ein ligro'a mimmenhu — 'nothing can be added to it and nothing taken away from it.' This is the ontological description of divine sovereignty over creation: God's acts are complete and unalterable. Ve-ha'Elohim asah she-yir'u milfanav — 'God has done it so that people would fear him.' The purpose of divine immutability is the fear of God (yir'ah, H3374) — the reverential awe that is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 1:7). God's unchangeability, rather than making him remote, creates the conditions for appropriate human response.
15: Mah-shehayah kebar hu — 'What is has already been.' The cyclical observation of 1:9 is restated but now in a theological frame: it is not merely a human observation about repetition but a characterization of how God relates to history. Ve-asher lih-yot kebar hayah ve-ha'Elohim yevaqesh et-nirdaf — 'and what is to be already has been. God seeks out what has been driven away.' The final phrase is the most difficult in this verse: et-nirdaf (H7291, the hunted/driven away/pursued one) — God 'seeks' what has been pushed aside, marginalized, or seemingly lost. Some read this as a recycling of the past; others as God pursuing justice for the driven-away (the oppressed, cf. 3:16). The theological claim is that God's sovereignty over time means nothing falls permanently outside his attention.
16: Ve-od ra'iti tahat hashemesh meqom ha-mishpat shamah ha-resha — 'Moreover, I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, even there was wickedness.' Mishpat (H4941, justice/judgment) and tzedeq (righteousness) are the two pillars of YHWH's throne (Ps 89:14); here both are perverted in the institutions (maqom, place) designed to embody them. The corruption of the court system — the place of justice becoming the place of wickedness — is the most socially devastating form of hevel: the system designed to protect the oppressed becomes a tool of oppression. The Teacher does not call for revolution; he observes and waits for v.17's resolution.
17: Amarthi ani be-libbi et-ha-tzadiq ve-et-ha-rasha yishpot ha'Elohim — 'I said in my heart, “God will judge the righteous and the wicked.”' The theological response to v.16's corruption is not despair but confidence in divine judgment. Ki et le-khol-chefetz ve-al kol-hama'aseh sham — 'for there is a time for every matter and for every work.' The word sham ('there') is puzzling: where is 'there'? Some read it as 'then' (temporal); others as a reference to the divine court. The appointed-time structure of the poem (3:1-8) returns: the corruption of human courts will have its appointed time of divine judgment. The et/ze-man structure that governs all things governs judgment too.
18: Amarthi ani be-libbi al-dibrat benei ha-adam — 'I said in my heart with regard to the children of man.' Dibrat (H1700) — rare word; 'on account of/concerning/for the sake of' — the Teacher's reflection is directed specifically at the human situation. Levar'am ha'Elohim ve-lir'ot she-hem-behemah hemah lahem — 'that God is testing them so they may see that they themselves are but animals.' The verb berar (H1305, to sift/test/purify) appears in a reflexive form: God is separating/testing humanity to show them something about themselves. The word behemah (beast/animal, H929) is the sharp term: the Teacher makes the unsentimental claim that from a sub-eschatological perspective, humans and beasts share the same creaturely mortality.
19: Ki miqreh benei ha-adam u-miqreh ha-behemah u-miqreh echad lahem — 'For what happens to the children of man is what happens to the beasts; the same thing happens to both.' The word miqreh (event/fate) appears three times in this verse — the repetition is emphatic. Ke-mot zeh ken mot zeh — 'As one dies, so dies the other.' The brevity of the construction heightens the impact: no expansion, no qualification. Ve-ruach echad le-khol — 'They all have the same breath/spirit.' Ruach echad (one spirit/breath) — the single ruach that gives life to both human and animal, and whose departure means death for both. U-motar ha-adam min-ha-behemah ayin — 'man has no advantage over the beasts' — motar (H4195, advantage/profit) is yet another word in the yitron family; from the perspective of mortality alone, there is no human advantage. Ki hakol havel — 'All is futility.' The only verse in this section that ends with havel alone, without 're'ut ruach.'
20: Hakol holekh el-maqom echad — 'All go to one place.' Maqom echad (one place) — the shared destination of all mortals: the grave, Sheol. The universality is total: not 'some' or 'most' but 'all.' Hakol hayah min-ha'afar ve-hakol shav el-ha'afar — 'All are from the dust, and all return to dust.' The echo of Genesis 3:19 is unmistakable (ki afar atah ve-el-afar tashuv: 'for you are dust, and to dust you shall return') — the Teacher is applying the curse of Gen 3 to the full range of created mortality. The Teacher does not cite Genesis, but his observation is rooted in the same creaturely condition that the fall confirmed.
21: Mi yode'a ruach benei ha-adam ha-olah hi lema'lah — 'Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward?' The mi yode'a ('who knows?') formula marks the limit of 'under the sun' knowledge. The question about the destiny of the human ruach is left open: goes upward (to God) or downward (like the animal's spirit, to the earth)? Ve-ruach ha-behemah hayoredet hi lemattah la'aretz — 'and the spirit of the beast goes downward to the earth?' The Teacher does not deny the resurrection — he identifies it as beyond the epistemological reach of sub-eschatological wisdom. The NT's announcement of resurrection is the answer to precisely this mi yode'a: the resurrection of Christ demonstrates that the human ruach does indeed have an upward destiny, because Christ's has shown the way (1 Cor 15:20).
22: Ve-ra'iti ki ein tov me-asher yismach ha-adam be-ma'asav — 'So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work.' The carpe diem counsel recurs, now grounded in the preceding meditation on mortality and divine judgment. Rejoicing in work is the positive response to the Teacher's epistemological limit: since 'who knows' what comes after, the portion available now — enjoyment in work — is the wise response. Ki hu helqo — 'for that is his portion.' Helqo (his portion) — the concept introduced at 2:10: the helq that actually belongs to the person, as opposed to the accumulated amal that passes to another. Ki mi yevi'ennu lir'ot bemah sheyih-yeh acharav — 'For who can bring him to see what will be after him?' The future is beyond sight; the present is available. This is not fatalism but realism about the limits of human knowledge.
